title: Recording Media, Photography Applications, and Personnel Certification
narrationScript: An inspection you cannot prove is an opinion. This lesson is about turning what you saw into a record that stands up. In photographs, in video, and in files that someone can trust years from now. Start with what every image needs. Identification. A weld number, a part number, something in the frame or inseparably tied to it. A scale, because a photograph without dimensional reference proves very little. And traceability. The date, the examiner, and a link to the report. Hold the camera square to the surface when dimensions matter, because shooting at an angle distorts them. That distortion is parallax, and it has embarrassed more than one inspector. Lighting makes or breaks technical photography. For welds and brazed joints, skim the light across the surface at a low angle. Raking light throws shadows from ripple, undercut, and toe geometry that direct flash flattens into nothing. For pressure vessels, shoot in sequence. An overall view, then a locator view with a landmark you can find again, then the close up. Years later, that sequence is what lets someone stand where you stood. Photograph the nameplate first, and the rest of the file anchors itself to the right vessel. Pumps and valves bring a different problem, machined and lapped surfaces that throw glare. Diffuse the light, bounce it, or move it off axis until the surface detail comes back. Photograph disassembly in order, so the record and the reassembly agree. Castings want low angle light to bring up surface texture, and repair excavations should be photographed with a gauge in the frame before they are welded shut. On forgings, oblique light makes laps stand out along flash lines and fillets. Microcircuits move you to a stereo microscope, typically ten to sixty power, with illumination chosen to kill hot spots on reflective pads, and a stage scale in the image so the magnification is on record. Handle that hardware with static protection, always. Now the rules. Structural welding codes and pressure codes expect examination records, and they say who must make them and how long they live. When access forces remote visual testing with cameras or borescopes, the codes are consistent in spirit. Prove the system first. Before the examination, demonstrate that the system resolves the required detail on a test target at the working distance and angle, and record the settings you used. If you annotate borescope video with audio, speak location, orientation, and time as you go, because video without position information is nearly useless a month later. Electronic media is where good inspections go to die when nobody thinks ahead. Use enough resolution and go easy on compression, because compression artifacts can hide an indication or invent one. Name files so they tie to the report and the weld map. Keep the original unedited file as the record. If an image needs brightening to read, do it on a copy and write down what you did. Back everything up, and remember that storage media age. Drives fail, tapes degrade, formats fade. Long retention means planned migration, not a box of disks in a drawer. Retention itself comes from the code or the contract, and for some equipment it means the life of the item. And the last piece of the record is you. Qualification and certification requirements follow this work everywhere. Your certification, your current vision examination, and your level define what you may perform, interpret, evaluate, and report. Auditors pull personnel records right next to the photographs, and a perfect image signed by a lapsed certificate fails the audit just the same. Keep both in order. Sign what you did, and only what you did, and the record you leave behind will speak for you long after you have left the site.

sections.heading: The Record Outlives the Inspection
sections.body:
Photographic records exist to do three jobs: provide **objective evidence** behind an accept/reject call, establish a **baseline** for future comparison, and let someone who was never on site understand exactly what you saw. Every technical image therefore needs:

- **Identification** — the weld number, part number, or item ID visible in the frame or inseparably tied to the file.
- **Scale** — a rule or calibrated reference beside the area of interest; an image without dimensional reference proves very little.
- **Traceability** — date, examiner, and a link to the report and procedure.

Technique basics carry most of the quality: hold the camera **square to the surface** when dimensions matter (an angled shot foreshortens — that is parallax error), fill the frame with the subject, confirm focus on site while re-shooting is still cheap, and note the lighting used. A photograph is an extension of the examination, so the same lighting discipline applies: if the procedure required 1000 lux and raking light to see the indication, the photograph needs equivalent conditions to show it.

sections.caption: A defensible weld photograph: camera square to the surface, raking light skimming the toe, and scale plus weld ID inside the frame.

sections.heading: Welds, Pressure Vessels, Pumps, and Valves
sections.body:
**Metal joining processes**: photograph welds and brazed joints with **raking light across the joint axis** so ripple, toe geometry, undercut, and the braze fillet line cast shadows; direct on-axis flash flattens exactly the relief you are documenting. Shoot before/after pairs for repairs, and macro shots of indications with the scale in the same plane as the indication.

**Pressure vessels**: use the **three-shot discipline** — an overall view, a locator view containing a findable landmark (nozzle, seam intersection, datum marking), then the close-up. Photograph the nameplate first so the whole file anchors to the right vessel. Interior shots need wide-angle coverage plus auxiliary lighting; watch white balance under mixed light sources so corrosion-product colors stay truthful.

**Pumps and valves**: photograph the disassembly sequence in order and mark orientation before teardown, so the record matches reassembly. Machined and lapped surfaces — seats, discs, wear rings, shaft sleeves — throw specular glare; diffuse the light, bounce it, or move it off-axis until surface detail returns. Document seating-band continuity on valves and cavitation patterns on impeller vanes with the scale in frame.

sections.heading: Castings, Forgings, and Microcircuits
sections.body:
**Castings** photograph poorly under flat light because the story is texture. Use low-angle light to raise the surface, photograph against the procedure's surface-comparator plate when roughness is the question, and always document repair excavations — with a depth gauge or scale in the cavity — before weld repair closes them forever.

**Forgings**: laps and seams follow flash lines, fillets, and corners; oblique light along those features makes the tight, curved indications visible. Where the procedure includes macro-etching, photograph the etched flow-line pattern with the part ID and an orientation reference in frame.

**Microcircuits and electronic assemblies** shift the scale: photography happens through a **stereo microscope**, typically 10–60×, examining solder fillets, wetting, bridging, lifted pads, and wire bonds. Illumination choice rules the result — ring lights and diffuse domes suppress the specular hot spots that bare pads and solder menisci throw under point sources. Include a stage micrometer or graticule in at least one frame so magnification is on the record, and handle everything with ESD protection: the inspection must not become the damage mechanism.

sections.caption: The life of an electronic VT record: capture with proven resolution, verify, identify, store with backup and integrity checks, then retain and migrate per the governing code or contract.

sections.heading: Codes and Audio/Video Records
sections.body:
Construction and structural welding codes — AWS-type structural codes, ASME-type pressure codes — expect examination results to be **recorded, attributable, and retained**. They state who makes the record, what it must contain, and how long it lives; the contract can extend all three.

For **remote VT** — video borescopes, crawler cameras, drones — the recurring code requirement is simple in spirit: **prove the system before you use it**. Demonstrate that the camera-monitor chain resolves the required detail (a fine line, lettering, or a specified test target) at the actual working distance, angle, and lighting, and record that demonstration. A system that cannot resolve the target does not perform the examination, no matter how good the footage looks.

**Audio annotation** turns raw video into a usable record: speak location, orientation, component ID, and time as you drive the probe, because thirty minutes of unlabeled bore footage is nearly worthless a month later. Log system settings, probe type, and lighting with the recording, and treat the video file with the same retention and traceability rules as a report page.

sections.caption: Record elements and the expectations codes and contracts typically attach to them — always verify the specific governing document.
rows: Part/weld ID, date, examiner, and report number traceable on or with every image
Resolution demonstrated on a test target at working distance and angle before examination
Unedited original retained; enhancement done on copies and documented
Set by code or contract — from several years to the life of the item
Certification and vision examination current; level matches the task performed

sections.heading: Electronic and Magnetic Media
sections.body:
Digital files are the dominant recording medium, and they fail in predictable ways:

- **Resolution and compression** — capture at adequate resolution and minimal compression; aggressive compression creates artifacts that can mask a fine indication or fabricate the appearance of one. Use the least-compressed format the system offers for record images.
- **Identification** — file names tied to the report, weld map, or item number; camera date/time set correctly so metadata supports, rather than contradicts, the report.
- **Integrity** — the **unedited original is the record**. Adjust brightness or crop only on copies, document the adjustment, and keep originals write-protected; checksums or controlled document systems prove nothing was altered.
- **Storage** — never leave the only copy on the camera card. Move files to a controlled server with backup the same day.
- **Media aging** — magnetic tape, optical disks, and hard drives all degrade, and formats become unreadable. Long retention periods require planned **migration** to current media, not a box of disks in a drawer.

Audio records — borescope narration, dictated field notes — follow the same rules: identified, retained, unedited.

sections.title: Certification Closes the Loop
sections.body: The record is only as good as the signature on it. Personnel qualification and certification — an employer written practice per SNT-TC-1A or ANSI/ASNT CP-189 style requirements, or NAS410 in aerospace — defines who may perform, interpret, evaluate, and **report** results. Certification records, current vision examinations, and the examiner's level are quality records that auditors pull right beside your photographs: a perfect image attached to a lapsed certification fails the audit. Sign what you did, at the level you hold, every time.

figures.title: Electronic VT Records: Capture to Retention

interactive.title: Document a Repair Weld for the Permanent File
interactive.intro: A code-vessel repair weld passed your direct visual examination, but one zone of marginal toe blending must be photographed for the permanent file. You have a digital camera, a small LED lamp, a flexible scale, and the weld map.

steps.prompt: The toe-blending detail barely shows under the shop's overhead flood lights. How do you light it for the photograph?
options: Use the camera's built-in flash aimed straight at the weld
Hold the LED lamp at a low angle, skimming light across the weld toe
Turn off all the lights and use a long exposure
steps.feedback: Raking light at a low angle throws shadows from surface relief, which is exactly what makes toe geometry visible. Direct on-axis flash flattens contrast and adds glare — the detail disappears.

steps.prompt: What belongs in the frame with the weld?
options: Nothing — keep the image clean for maximum clarity
A scale and the weld number or ID tag placed beside the area of interest
Your initials written on the weld in permanent marker
steps.feedback: A scale gives dimensional reference and the weld ID ties the image to the weld map and report. Without them, the photograph proves very little — and marking the weld surface itself is not yours to do.

steps.prompt: How do you shoot the area?
options: One tight macro shot of the toe only
An overall view, a locator view with a findable landmark, then the close-up
Twenty shots from random angles to be safe
steps.feedback: The overall–locator–close-up sequence lets a reviewer find the exact spot on the vessel years later and then study the detail. A lone macro shot can never be located again; random shots bury the evidence.

steps.prompt: Back at the desk, the close-up looks slightly dark. What do you do with the files?
options: Brighten a working copy, document the adjustment, and retain the unedited original linked to the report number
Brighten the original in place and delete the dark version to avoid confusion
Discard the photographs and rely on the written description
steps.feedback: The unedited original is the record. Enhancement happens on a copy and gets documented, so nobody can claim the image was manipulated — and the report keeps its photographic evidence.

checkQuestions.question: Why does a scale belong in the frame of an indication photograph?
options: It makes the image look more official
It provides dimensional reference so the image supports later evaluation
Codes prohibit photographs without borders
It corrects the white balance
checkQuestions.explanation: Without a dimensional reference in the image plane, nobody can later judge the size of what was photographed — the scale is what turns a picture into evidence.

checkQuestions.question: The best lighting to make weld-toe undercut visible in a photograph is:
options: Direct on-axis flash
Overhead shop flood lights alone
Low-angle raking light across the weld axis
No light and a long exposure
checkQuestions.explanation: Raking light throws shadows from surface relief such as undercut and ripple. On-axis flash fills and flattens those shadows, hiding exactly what the photograph is supposed to show.

checkQuestions.question: Glare hiding detail on a lapped valve seat photograph is best controlled by:
options: Diffusing the light or moving it off-axis
Increasing the flash power
Zooming out and cropping later
Converting the image to black and white
checkQuestions.explanation: Machined and lapped surfaces reflect specularly; softening the light with a diffuser or moving it off the lens axis removes the hot spot. More flash power makes the glare worse.

checkQuestions.question: Before a video borescope system is used for a code remote visual examination, you must:
options: Paint the part surface white for contrast
Set the date stamp and nothing else
Fully charge the battery and begin
Demonstrate the system resolves the required detail on a test target at the working distance and angle
checkQuestions.explanation: Codes require the remote system to demonstrate resolution capability — typically on a fine line, lettering, or specified target — under the actual working conditions before the examination counts.

checkQuestions.question: Your record close-up is underexposed. The compliant handling is:
options: Brighten the original file in place
Delete it and describe the indication in words only
Brighten a copy, document the adjustment, and retain the unedited original
Crop out the dark area and submit the remainder
checkQuestions.explanation: The unedited original is the record of what the camera captured. Enhancements are performed on copies and documented, preserving the integrity of the evidence chain.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II Supplement §5.2–5.5
Structural welding and pressure codes on examination records and remote VT (generic, e.g., AWS structural codes, ASME Section V Article 9)
SNT-TC-1A, ANSI/ASNT CP-189, and NAS410 personnel certification frameworks (generic reference)
